name: pool-route
description: PreToolUse(Bash) pool auto-router + SessionStart routing-log snapshot, implemented in hexa-lang (`_pool_route.hexa`, invoked via `hexa run`). When the `pool` CLI's roster (`~/.pool/pool.json`) has ≥1 enabled host and a Bash command matches the heavy classifier (make · cargo · npm · gradle · pytest · go build · swift build · docker build · gcc/g++/clang/clang++/cc/c++ · nvidia-smi · train · hexa kick/drill/loop/cc/run/build · aprime_cc · `find $HOME/core/anima` big-tree scan · …) or a root-needing command, the command is rewritten via the hook `updatedInput` field to run on a pool host over ssh (`ssh <host> 'cd <workdir> && <cmd>'`) — transparent dispatch, not a suggestion. Routing is EXPLICIT-classifier only (0.6.0): the 0.5.7 load-escalation gate — which promoted any non-trivial cmd to heavy when summed non-claude CPU exceeded 150% — was removed because on a multi-core Mac that threshold trips constantly and shipped local-bound git/hexa-run/smoke commands to Linux hosts where their paths don't exist. Local-bound guard (0.6.0, extended 0.6.9): `git`/`gh` commands, any `/Users/`·`/home/` absolute-path literal, and any `~/.`·`$HOME/.` home-dotstate reference always run local — the dotstate forms (`~/.zsh_history`·`~/.hexa-cache`·`~/.pool`·`~/.sidecar`·`~/.config`·…) are per-host hidden state that is NOT user-synced, so routing them to a pool host reads the WRONG host's history/cache; `~/core/...` synced workdirs are excluded and keep routing (the `find ~/core/anima` big-tree pattern still dispatches). Sidecar plugin pin (0.6.8): cmds carrying `$CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT` or `$CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA` literals always run local — the literals resolve via the dispatching bash to the workstation's plugin cache, which only exists on the local Mac; routing them to a pool host fails (cache absent). Trusted surface (sidecar slash commands like `/quota:quota`, `/pool:pool`, `/check`, `/end`, `/ship`, `/inject`, `/secret`, … all match by writing convention @D s3), so no sign gate. Local-bound sign gate (0.6.4): within the absolute-path exemption, HEAVY invocations (hexa CLI any verb · direct `hexa.real` · python/python3/py · gcc/g++/clang/clang++/cc/c++ · `bash`/`sh <script>`) now require a fresh `local` sign-off token — they were the canonical mac fork-storm trigger (multi-session dispatch-cache races + re-fork on every env-hash miss). USER mints with `! sidecar sign local` in the TUI prompt (5min token, mints freely via `!` bang); the token file `~/.sidecar/signs/local.sign` holds the mint epoch. Without a fresh token the hook emits `permissionDecision: deny` with an instruction trailer. Bare absolute-path reads (ls/cat/grep/find/...) stay free — the gate narrows to interpreters/compilers only. HOTFIX 0.6.5: (a) `_local_heavy_interp` now matches FIRST-TOKEN basename instead of `_has_word("hexa")` — 0.6.4 mis-matched `hexa` inside path strings like `/Users/.../hexa-lang/foo.txt`, gating bare reads like `cat <path>`. (b) `hexa run` + `hexa build` added to the heavy classifier — 0.6.4 missed them, so they ran local on the mac instead of routing to the pool (= mac fork-storm trigger when multi-session). The two together close the false-positive AND false-negative paths. macOS pool-host enable (0.6.6 — supersedes 0.6.1 Zero-macOS-offload): macOS pool hosts (e.g. `mini` with `os: macos`) are now AUTO-DISPATCH candidates for macOS-only commands (swift build · xcodebuild · codesign · …). The 0.6.1 blanket exclusion left compile-heavy work (swift-frontend can drive 85%+ CPU on the workstation) stuck on the local Mac even when an enabled `mini` was idle. New policy: (a) macOS-only commands route to macOS pool hosts; (b) Linux-only commands route to Linux pool hosts (unchanged); (c) general-heavy commands round-robin across Linux pool hosts only — the workstation Mac stays protected from generic build/test fan-out and is reached only when a macOS-capability marker explicitly demands it. Heavy-classified commands MUST use pool resources: the hook iterates every eligible host through a preflight (workdir present + the command's toolchain actually runnable — `command -v hexa` + `test -x build/hexa_interp` + `hexa <verb> --help` for hexa, `command -v <compiler>` for C/C++ — 0.6.13), routes to the first that passes, and if ALL fail emits a `permissionDecision: deny` with a per-host failure breakdown — never silently falls back to local. `hexa kick`/`drill`/`loop`/`cc` get `HEXA_LANG=$HOME/core/hexa-lang`; other hexa commands get `HEXA_LANG=$PWD`. Each routing decision appends one JSON line to `~/.pool/route-log.jsonl` (capped at 100); SessionStart reads the last 5 back as additionalContext. Cloud pin (0.6.10): `hexa cloud *` always runs local — it is ITSELF a Mac-local-only remote-dispatch tool (needs the local hexa build + stdlib/cloud), and its post-`--` remote argv routinely carries heavy words (nvidia-smi · train.log · make) that tripped the classifier, non-deterministically shipping `hexa cloud exec <pod> -- ...` to a Linux pool host with no `cloud` subcommand. Double-routing a dispatcher is incoherent. Worktree→canonical-root fallback (0.6.10): a heavy command in a git LINKED worktree outside $HOME (e.g. /tmp/wt-x — the standard stdlib/SSCHA isolation pattern) previously DENIED ('cwd outside $HOME'); it now resolves the MAIN checkout via `git worktree list --porcelain` (listed first) and, when that root is under $HOME (a synced ~/core workdir), mirrors it instead. This runs ONLY in the previously-denying branch, so it cannot regress a routing command — it strictly rescues worktree dispatch. C/C++ compiler routing (0.6.11): the C/C++ compiler drivers (`gcc` · `g++` · `clang` · `clang++` · `cc` · `c++`) join the heavy classifier — a C/C++ compile is heavy local compute, same workstation-protection rationale as `make`/`cmake`/`cargo`. They carry no macOS-capability marker, so they fall into the general-heavy bucket → Linux-only round-robin (the Mac stays the workstation, per @D s12). Word-matched (not first-token) so a zsh-snapshot-wrapped invocation is still caught; `cc` is the only 2-char driver but git/gh + absolute-path commands already returned local above, so a coincidental bare `cc` token is unlikely to reach the classifier. Absolute-path compiler sign gate (0.6.12): the C/C++ compiler drivers also join the local-bound sign gate within the absolute-path exemption — an absolute-path compile (e.g. `/usr/bin/gcc /Users/…/x.c`) is the same mac fork-storm trigger as a heavy interpreter, so it now requires a fresh `local` sign-off token (`! sidecar sign local`) just like hexa/python/sh; relative-path compiles in synced workdirs still route to the pool unchanged. First-token basename match keeps it precise — only a real compiler invocation, never a bare read mentioning `cc` inside a path. Toolchain preflight + akida exclusion (0.6.13): (a) pi5-akida is excluded from the auto-dispatch candidate set entirely (@D s8 — anima-only; reached ONLY via explicit `pool on pi5-akida`, the pool CLI not this hook). (b) the host preflight now verifies the command's ACTUAL toolchain, not just the workdir — every `hexa` verb probes `command -v hexa` + `test -x ~/core/hexa-lang/build/hexa_interp` + `hexa <verb> --help`, and a C/C++ compile probes `command -v <compiler>`; a host that fails is SKIPPED so the round-robin moves to the next eligible host instead of routing-then-failing (the ubu-1 broken-hexa trap). Known limit (@D d6): a binary that passes `--help` but segfaults at runtime is not caught (full exec probe deferred). mini-equal toggle (0.7.0 — supersedes the 0.6.6 Linux-only-general policy when opted in): `pool mini-equal on` writes `~/.pool/mini-equal = "on"`, which opts macOS pool hosts (mini) INTO the OS-agnostic general-heavy round-robin as equal workers alongside the Linux hosts. Reached ONLY in the general-heavy branch (OS-agnostic work) — is_linux commands (apt · elf · CUDA) still route Linux-only and never land on a macOS host, so the toggle only redistributes portable work. Default OFF (marker absent or != "on") preserves zero-macOS-offload (@D s12). This is NOT an in-guard opt-out (@D s11): it OPENS routing to an otherwise-idle host, it does not DISABLE a safety guard. Single local-execution gate (0.7.2): a fresh `local` sign token (`! sidecar sign local`, 5min) now forces EVERY command to run local — the one deliberate, user-minted 'run here, do NOT route' path, checked at the top of the router before classification. Consolidates local-exec control to one sign-gated channel: when the router mis-classifies a local-bound command (a diagnostic `ps`/`awk`, a local-fs fix) and ships it to a peer host, the user signs ONCE and all routing is suppressed for the window. Subsumes the abs-path-heavy sign gate (when signed, the top-level check returns first; when unsigned, that gate still bites — fork-storm protection unchanged). A sanctioned, time-boxed, user-minted token — NOT an env-var/config opt-out (@D s11). Env-prefix + wrapper hardening (0.7.3): `_local_heavy_interp` now strips POSIX env-var prefix (`POOL_DISABLE=1`/`SIDECAR_POOL_DISABLE=1`/`FOO=bar`/…, any IDENT=val — not specific vars, parser correctness only) AND known leading-position wrappers (`env` · `exec` · `nice` · `timeout` · `command` · `sudo` · `nohup` · `stdbuf` · `ionice`, with per-wrapper option-arg awareness — `nice -n 10`/`env -u VAR`/`timeout -k 5 60`/`stdbuf -oL`/…) AND a leading backslash escape (`\hexa`) before reading the real verb. Pre-0.7.3 any env-var prefix OR any of those wrappers silently ALLOW'd abs-path heavy invocations, breaking sign-local's single-gate integrity (verified leak across 22 vectors; all closed post-fix). `eval 'quoted shell string'` remains out of scope (its arg requires a real shell parser). Node-package installer pin (0.7.4): npm/pnpm/yarn run LOCAL — they read package.json + write node_modules in CWD, so routing them to a pool host (where the CWD usually doesn't exist) failed preflight on every host and denied the command. Removed from heavy_words and added to the local-bound early-_allow chain. Routing-rate baseline counters (0.7.5 — POOL-OFFLOAD m1): every meaningful classifier verdict increments one of 4 counters in `~/.pool/route-counters.tally` (routed · local_bound · light_allowed · heavy_failed) so `/check` can report the routing-rate baseline (% of bash invocations actually offloaded to the pool vs stayed local). Non-classifier exits (non-Bash · empty cmd · MARK · heredoc · already-ssh) are NOT counted, so the ratio reflects real routing decisions. Race-tolerant under concurrent sessions (occasional lossy increment is acceptable for baseline measurement). OS-capability filter + round-robin load balance. NO guard opt-out by design.
